Rugby stadium in Edinburgh, Scotland

Murrayfield Stadium

**Murrayfield Stadium** is a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") located in the [area of the same name](/wiki/Murrayfield "Murrayfield") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland. The stadium is owned by the [Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union") (SRU) which has its headquarters based at the stadium, and is the national stadium of the [Scotland national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team"). With a seating capacity of 67,144, it is the largest stadium in Scotland, the fifth largest in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and the twenty-second largest [in Europe](/wiki/List_of_European_stadiums_by_capacity "List of European stadiums by capacity").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

It officially opened on 21 March 1925 with a game between Scotland and [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The game was won by Scotland who came out victorious following a [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(rugby_union) "Grand Slam (rugby union)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The stadium hosts most of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team")'s home test matches and the *Scottish Hydro Electric Cup* final, as well as [URC](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup") matches.

Although primarily a rugby union stadium, Murrayfield has in the past hosted [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), [rugby league](/wiki/Challenge_Cup "Challenge Cup") and [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, as well as numerous music concerts. Currently, the stadium is known as **Scottish Gas Murrayfield Stadium** for sponsorship reasons.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Purchase of land

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Purchase of land")]

The SRU identified 19 acres of land at Murrayfield, purchasing this from Edinburgh Polo Club at Murrayfield, having raised money through [debentures](/wiki/Debenture_(sport) "Debenture (sport)").[[4]](#cite_note-Herald_14Mar1995-4) A stand and three embankments were constructed, which took two years.[[4]](#cite_note-Herald_14Mar1995-4) Previous internationals had been played at [Inverleith](/wiki/Inverleith_Sports_Ground "Inverleith Sports Ground")[[5]](#cite_note-Scotsman_28Jan2015-5) but it was not large enough to cope with the increasing number of spectators.[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Arthur Sellers](/wiki/Arthur_Sellers_(Scottish_cricketer) "Arthur Sellers (Scottish cricketer)") was the stadium's first groundsman, having previously prepared the pitches at Inverleith.[[7]](#cite_note-7) On 21 March 1925 [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") were the first team to visit Murrayfield, with 70,000 people watching [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") beat them to win their first [Five Nations Championship](/wiki/Five_Nations_Championship "Five Nations Championship") [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(Rugby_Union) "Grand Slam (Rugby Union)").[[5]](#cite_note-Scotsman_28Jan2015-5)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Usage during WWII

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Usage during WWII")]

During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") the ground at Murrayfield was offered to the nation and was taken over by the [Royal Army Service Corps](/wiki/Royal_Army_Service_Corps "Royal Army Service Corps") and used as a supply depot. During the war years the [armed forces](/wiki/British_armed_forces "British armed forces") sports authorities managed to arrange two Scotland v. England services internationals each year, on a home-and-away basis. Scotland's home matches were played at Inverleith for the first two years with a return to Murrayfield in 1944 after that ground's derequisition.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Recent history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Murrayfield_Stadium_2005-05-13.jpg/250px-Murrayfield_Stadium_2005-05-13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_Stadium_2005-05-13.jpg)

Exterior of Murrayfield, May 2005

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg/250px-Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)

Interior of Murrayfield depicting the [national flag of Scotland](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland"), the *Saltire*, in the stands

Murrayfield's record attendance of 104,000 was set on 1 March 1975 when Scotland defeated [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") 12–10 during the [1975 Five Nations Championship](/wiki/1975_Five_Nations_Championship "1975 Five Nations Championship").[[9]](#cite_note-9) This attendance stood as a world record until 1999, and remains a European record.[[5]](#cite_note-Scotsman_28Jan2015-5) The East stand was built in 1983.[[10]](#cite_note-Herald_growing_great_5Feb1994-10)

In October 1991 another debenture scheme was launched, to finance a more comprehensive redevelopment of the West Stand.[[11]](#cite_note-Herald_debenture_5Feb1994-11) The new West Stand was designed with a 48-metre cantilever roof.[[12]](#cite_note-Herald_13Jan1993-12) The old West Stand housed a museum, but this was not replaced and plans for a new visitor centre were shelved when the game turned professional.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first phase was completed in January 1993 with the new north and south stands opening.[[12]](#cite_note-Herald_13Jan1993-12) In February 1994 the centre section of the new West Stand opened.[[10]](#cite_note-Herald_growing_great_5Feb1994-10) The work was carried out by [Tilbury Douglas](/wiki/Tilbury_Douglas "Tilbury Douglas").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1994, Murrayfield completed a £50-million renovation where floodlights were installed for the first time.

In October 2012, SRU chief executive Mark Dodson told the BBC that it was actively seeking a name sponsor for Murrayfield:[[15]](#cite_note-15)

> The single biggest piece of our inventory is our national stadium. We would like to see if we can monetise that. It would be crazy for us not to look at using our single biggest piece of inventory to drive revenue. We want to get the right price for it.

In addition, Dodson indicated that the SRU was actively seeking a site for a completely new stadium with a capacity of 10,000 to 15,000 as a future home for Edinburgh Rugby.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The pitch was damaged by [nematodes](/wiki/Nematode "Nematode") in the lead up to the 2013 autumn internationals. This led the SRU to replace the grass with a [Desso surface](/wiki/Desso_GrassMaster "Desso GrassMaster") from the start of the 2014 season.[[17]](#cite_note-17) A naming rights deal with [BT](/wiki/British_Telecom "British Telecom") was agreed in May 2014, resulting in the stadium being officially named as the BT Murrayfield Stadium.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Scottish Rugby and [Scottish Gas](/wiki/Scottish_Gas "Scottish Gas") agreed a new five-year partnership deal starting in July 2023. This will result in the creation of a Club & Community Net Zero Fund worth £2 million to ensure Scottish clubs can save on energy bills and carbon emissions. Scottish Gas will also invest in the women's game through shirt and stadium sponsorship, resulting in an official name change for the stadium to Scottish Gas Murrayfield.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Location")]

Murrayfield is located next to [Murrayfield Ice Rink](/wiki/Murrayfield_Ice_Rink "Murrayfield Ice Rink"), Murrayfield Curling Rink, and is close to [Edinburgh Zoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Zoo "Edinburgh Zoo"). It is named after the area of Edinburgh it is located in, [Murrayfield](/wiki/Murrayfield "Murrayfield"). There are two cricket pitches in the immediate vicinity at Roseburn Park used by Murrayfield DAFS CC and four rugby pitches owned by the SRU which were used by teams including the amateur club [Murrayfield Wanderers RFC](/wiki/Murrayfield_Wanderers_RFC "Murrayfield Wanderers RFC"). Wanderers and their predecessors had played there since 1902 but were asked to vacate in 2018 as the governing body had plans to develop the land.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Wanderers moved their training base to Roseburn but continued to hire the Murrayfield pitches for some matches.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Buses")]

The stadium is served by [Lothian Bus](/wiki/Lothian_Buses "Lothian Buses") services 12, 22, 26, 31 and the [Airlink 100](/wiki/Airport_bus "Airport bus") along Corstorphine Road.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Post-match traffic congestion is common along this route.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rail")]

Despite the line running adjacent to the stadium, the closest railway station to the stadium is [Haymarket](/wiki/Haymarket_railway_station "Haymarket railway station"), which lies a mile to the East.

Interchange with the [Edinburgh Trams](/wiki/Edinburgh_Trams "Edinburgh Trams") is available at Haymarket, [Edinburgh Park](/wiki/Edinburgh_Park_railway_station "Edinburgh Park railway station") and [Edinburgh Gateway](/wiki/Edinburgh_Gateway_railway_station "Edinburgh Gateway railway station") stations. [Edinburgh Waverley](/wiki/Edinburgh_Waverley_railway_station "Edinburgh Waverley railway station") is a short walk from the [St Andrew Square](/wiki/St_Andrew_Square,_Edinburgh "St Andrew Square, Edinburgh") tram stop.

### Tram

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Tram")]

[Murrayfield Stadium tram stop](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium_tram_stop "Murrayfield Stadium tram stop") is located adjacent to the stadium entrance turnstiles on Roseburn Street. Access to the platform is by a flight of stairs or lift. As part of crowd-management measures, ticketing machines are situated at the bottom of the staircase and not the platform.

## Rugby union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rugby union")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/MurrayfieldStadium2002.jpg/250px-MurrayfieldStadium2002.jpg)](/wiki/File:MurrayfieldStadium2002.jpg)

Murrayfield Stadium in 2002.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Murrayfield_Stadium%2C_Edinburgh_%282%29_%288074639937%29_%282%29.jpg/250px-Murrayfield_Stadium%2C_Edinburgh_%282%29_%288074639937%29_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_Stadium,_Edinburgh_(2)_(8074639937)_(2).jpg)

Interior of Murrayfield

Murrayfield is used for most [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") international [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") matches, with all Scotland's [Six Nations](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") home games being played in the stadium. The stadium also hosted all of [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby")'s matches between 1996 and January 2017. (For Pro14 matches, only the lower tier of the East Stand is typically used.)
Since February 2018 all Edinburgh matches are once again held at Murrayfield; with work now finished on the construction of a [new 7,800 capacity stadium on the back pitches](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby_Stadium "Edinburgh Rugby Stadium") to host Edinburgh Rugby from the start of the 2021/22 season.

From [2007](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_IRB_Sevens_World_Series "2006–07 IRB Sevens World Series") to [2011](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_IRB_Sevens_World_Series "2010–11 IRB Sevens World Series"), Murrayfield hosted the [Edinburgh 7s](/wiki/Edinburgh_Sevens "Edinburgh Sevens"), then the final event in the annual IRB Sevens World Series (now known as [SVNS](/wiki/SVNS "SVNS")) in [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"), but that tournament was moved to [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") starting in [2012](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_IRB_Sevens_World_Series "2011–12 IRB Sevens World Series") due to low attendance. Murrayfield hosted select matches from the [2007 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup"). The stadium also hosted the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") Final in [2005](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Heineken_Cup "2004–05 Heineken Cup"), when [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") beat [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français") 18–12, again in [2009](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Heineken_Cup "2008–09 Heineken Cup"), with Leinster defeating Leicester 19–16 and finally in [2017](/w/index.php?title=2017_European_Champions_Cup_Final&action=edit&redlink=1 "2017 European Champions Cup Final (page does not exist)") with Saracens defeating Clermont 28–17.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Rugby league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Rugby league")]

Although primarily a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") stadium, Murrayfield hosted the [Rugby League](/wiki/Rugby_League "Rugby League") [Challenge Cup](/wiki/Challenge_Cup "Challenge Cup") Finals of [2000](/wiki/2000_Challenge_Cup "2000 Challenge Cup") and [2002](/wiki/2002_Challenge_Cup "2002 Challenge Cup"). The stadium hosted rugby league's [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") [Magic Weekend](/wiki/Magic_Weekend "Magic Weekend") in 2009. The record for a rugby league attendance at the stadium is 67,247 for the 2000 Challenge Cup Final.

## Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other sports")]

### Association football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Association football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Murrayfield_cs.JPG/250px-Murrayfield_cs.JPG)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_cs.JPG)

[Hearts](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C.") v [Sparta Prague](/wiki/AC_Sparta_Prague "AC Sparta Prague") at Murrayfield during the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_UEFA_Cup "2006–07 UEFA Cup"), 2006

Murrayfield has also hosted [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches. In December 2003, the SRU board agreed to let local [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") side [Hearts](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C.") lease the stadium for match days.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Later that month, [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") confirmed that Hearts' own ground, [Tynecastle](/wiki/Tynecastle_Stadium "Tynecastle Stadium") did not meet the minimum criteria for European matches from the 2004–05 season.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Hearts used Murrayfield as their home venue for European matches for three years[[27]](#cite_note-27) until Hearts made adjustments to Tynecastle that made it compliant with UEFA regulations.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Additionally, both Hearts and Edinburgh neighbours [Hibernian](/wiki/Hibernian_F.C. "Hibernian F.C.") have played preseason friendlies against [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") at Murrayfield.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Almost 58,000 people attended to watch Hearts play Barcelona in July 2007, recording the largest crowd at an association football match in Edinburgh for 51 years.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In 2014, Glasgow club [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") played two qualifying matches at the stadium due to [Celtic Park](/wiki/Celtic_Park "Celtic Park") being unavailable because of Glasgow's hosting of the [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games").[[32]](#cite_note-lfc-32) Hearts again used the stadium for home games during the [2017–18 Scottish Premiership](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Scottish_Premiership "2017–18 Scottish Premiership"), due to the delays in construction of a new main stand at Tynecastle.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Murrayfield hosted one of the [2018–19 Scottish League Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Scottish_League_Cup "2018–19 Scottish League Cup") semi-finals, played between Hearts and Celtic, in October 2018.[[32]](#cite_note-lfc-32) That match attracted an attendance of 61,161, the second-largest ever recorded for a football match in Edinburgh.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In July 2019, Murrayfield hosted a [pre-season friendly](/wiki/Pre-season_friendly "Pre-season friendly") between [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") and [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli"), that attracted a crowd of 65,442 which was the highest attendance of fans at a football match in Scotland since the [1989 Scottish Cup Final](/wiki/1989_Scottish_Cup_Final "1989 Scottish Cup Final").[[35]](#cite_note-35) On 19 July 2023, [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") faced [Olympique Lyonnais](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais") at Murrayfield in front of a crowd of 48,484.[[36]](#cite_note-36) On 20 March 2024, it was announced that Manchester United would return for the second successive year to play a pre-season match at Murrayfield, taking on [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") on 20 July 2024.[[37]](#cite_note-37) in front of 56,574

### American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: American football")]

Murrayfield has played host to [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") and was one of two home venues for the now defunct [Scottish Claymores](/wiki/Scottish_Claymores "Scottish Claymores") in the [NFL Europa](/wiki/NFL_Europa "NFL Europa") between 1995 and 2004, the other being [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park") in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"). Additionally, it hosted [World Bowl '96](/wiki/World_Bowl_%2796 "World Bowl '96") on 23 June 1996. It has been mentioned[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] as a potential future host site for the [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series"), should the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") add future games outside the series' current permanent home, [Tottenham Hotspur Stadium](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium "Tottenham Hotspur Stadium") in [London](/wiki/London "London"). Two other London stadiums, [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") and [Twickenham Stadium](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium"), are the only other grounds in Britain to host NFL matches.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murrayfield_Stadium&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/The_Rolling_Stones_Concert_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5804459.jpg/250px-The_Rolling_Stones_Concert_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5804459.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Rolling_Stones_Concert_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5804459.jpg)

Murrayfield during a concert held by [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), 2018

[David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") played to a capacity crowd of 47,000 people on 28 June 1983.[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Simple Minds](/wiki/Simple_Minds "Simple Minds") were scheduled to play at the stadium in 1989, but pulled out because of [Jim Kerr](/wiki/Jim_Kerr "Jim Kerr")'s objections to the venue's administrators allowing Scottish rugby players to attend the sport's centenary celebrations in South Africa, which was then still under apartheid.[[40]](#cite_note-40) On 30 June 1996, [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner") played at Murrayfield as part of her [Wildest Dreams Tour](/wiki/Wildest_Dreams_Tour "Wildest Dreams Tour"). In September 1997 [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") played at Murrayfield as part of their [Popmart Tour](/wiki/Popmart_Tour "Popmart Tour").[[41]](#cite_note-41) On 3 June 1999, [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") played to 51,000 on their [No Security Tour](/wiki/No_Security_Tour "No Security Tour").[[42]](#cite_note-42) On 8 July 1999 [Celine Dion](/wiki/Celine_Dion "Celine Dion") performed her [Let's Talk About Love World Tour](/wiki/Let%27s_Talk_About_Love_World_Tour "Let's Talk About Love World Tour") as she sold out the full venue of 67,000, on her first ever show in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). In July 2005, Murrayfield hosted the final [Live 8](/wiki/Live_8 "Live 8") concert, [Edinburgh 50,000 – The Final Push](/wiki/Edinburgh_50,000_%E2%80%93_The_Final_Push "Edinburgh 50,000 – The Final Push"), with performances including [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown_(musician) "James Brown (musician)"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas_(band) "Texas (band)") and [The Proclaimers](/wiki/The_Proclaimers "The Proclaimers"). [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") played a sold-out show on 17 June 2009, as part of their world tour. Some antisocial behaviour at this event affected the stadium's licensing arrangements when they were reviewed a few months later.[[43]](#cite_note-43) This was the last time Oasis would play a concert in Scotland[[44]](#cite_note-44) and the second time they had played the stadium, the first being on their [Standing on the Shoulder of Giants Tour](/wiki/Standing_on_the_Shoulder_of_Giants_Tour "Standing on the Shoulder of Giants Tour") in 2000.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[Bon Jovi](/wiki/Bon_Jovi "Bon Jovi") performed at the stadium on 22 June 2011 as part of their tour.[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(entertainer) "Madonna (entertainer)") performed to a sell-out crowd of 52,160 on 21 July 2012 as part of [the MDNA Tour](/wiki/The_MDNA_Tour "The MDNA Tour"). On 3 June 2014, [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction") performed to over 64,000 fans at Murrayfield as part of their [Where We Are Stadium Tour](/wiki/Where_We_Are_Tour_(One_Direction) "Where We Are Tour (One Direction)"). [Foo Fighters](/wiki/Foo_Fighters "Foo Fighters") performed at Murrayfield Stadium as part of their [Sonic Highways World Tour](/wiki/Sonic_Highways_World_Tour "Sonic Highways World Tour") on 8 September 2015. The band were originally supposed to play Murrayfield on 23 June 2015 but this was cancelled and rescheduled after [Dave Grohl](/wiki/Dave_Grohl "Dave Grohl") broke his foot at European Festival that same month.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The [Spice Girls](/wiki/Spice_Girls "Spice Girls") performed to over 55,000 fans on 8 June 2019 as part of their [Spice World tour](/wiki/Spice_World_-_2019_Tour "Spice World - 2019 Tour"), it was their first Scottish concert in 21 years. On May 20, 2023, [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") became the first Black woman since [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner") in 1996 to perform at the stadium in front of a sold-out audience of 55,000 people as part of her triumphant [Renaissance World Tour](/wiki/Renaissance_World_Tour "Renaissance World Tour"). [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") broke the all time Scottish concert attendance record 3 times when [The Eras Tour](/wiki/The_Eras_Tour "The Eras Tour") came to Murrayfield for 3 nights on 7, 8 and 9 June 2024, playing to 73,000 people each night, and nearly 220,000 people over the 3 nights.
